headline: Colorado's clean energy surge: from 2.6% to 53% in two decades
meta_description: Colorado now generates 53% of its electricity from wind, solar, and hydro—a remarkable jump from just 2.6% in 2004.
summary_short: Colorado has reached a watershed moment in its energy transition, with renewable sources now powering more than half the state's grid.
body:
In less than a decade, Colorado transformed its electricity supply. Wind, solar, and hydroelectric power now account for 53% of the state's electricity generation—a stunning leap from 43% just one year ago.

The trajectory tells an even more dramatic story. In 2004, renewables provided merely 2.6% of Colorado's power. Today's 53% represents a 20-fold increase across two decades, suggesting an accelerating shift rather than steady progress.

This milestone reflects both technological maturation and policy commitment. Colorado's abundant sunshine and wind resources have become increasingly viable, with solar and wind installations becoming cheaper and more efficient. The state's geography—home to the Rocky Mountains and vast plains—provides ideal conditions for both water and wind generation.

The clean energy milestone matters beyond statistics. It signals that large-scale transitions away from fossil fuels are achievable, offering a roadmap for other regions navigating the energy transition.
